parameters.jsCode:
const data = $input.first().json;
const { topic, difficulty, duration_min, duration_sec, num_clips, word_target } = data;
const topic_research = data.topic_research || '';

// Build scene time breakdown — each clip is 5s max but last clip may be shorter
const scenes = [];
for (let i = 0; i < num_clips; i++) {
  const startSec = i * 5;
  const endSec   = Math.min(startSec + 5, duration_sec);
  const segSec   = endSec - startSec;
  const mm = s => Math.floor(s/60) + ':' + String(s%60).padStart(2,'0');
  scenes.push('Scene ' + (i+1) + ' (' + mm(startSec) + ' - ' + mm(endSec) + ', ' + segSec + 's, ~' + Math.round(segSec * 2.5) + ' words)');
}
const sceneList = scenes.join('
  ');

const totalScenes = num_clips;
const cameraStages = [];
for (let i = 0; i < totalScenes; i++) {
  let stage;
  if (i === 0) stage = 'Scene ' + (i+1) + ': WIDE SHOT — establish full apparatus, show context';
  else if (i === totalScenes - 1) stage = 'Scene ' + (i+1) + ': EXTREME CLOSE-UP — final result detail';
  else if (i === 1) stage = 'Scene ' + (i+1) + ': MEDIUM SHOT — move toward the key component';
  else stage = 'Scene ' + (i+1) + ': CLOSE-UP — show the specific physics action';
  cameraStages.push(stage);
}
const cameraProgression = cameraStages.join('
  ');

const prompt = 'You are a physics documentary producer creating a ' + duration_min + '-minute YouTube Short (' + duration_sec + ' seconds) that must feel like a REAL physics explanation with matching simulation visuals.

TOPIC: ' + topic + '
DIFFICULTY: ' + difficulty + '
TARGET NARRATION: ~' + word_target + ' words (spoken at ~2.5 words/second = ' + duration_sec + ' seconds)
VIDEO CLIPS NEEDED: exactly ' + num_clips + ' clips, each 5 seconds (9:16 vertical mobile format)

TOPIC RESEARCH CONTEXT (use this to write accurate visual prompts):
' + topic_research + '

Use the above research to ensure each visual_prompt shows the EXACT scenario described — real equipment, real phenomena, real visual details from this specific topic.

SCENE BREAKDOWN (each clip matches one scene):
  ' + sceneList + '

CAMERA PROGRESSION (follow this for visual continuity):
  ' + cameraProgression + '

' +
'══════════════════════════════════════════
NARRATION RULES:
- Exactly ~' + word_target + ' words total — NOT more, NOT less
- The narration is a COMPLETE STORY with a clear beginning, middle, and end
- STRUCTURE (map to scenes proportionally):
  Scene 1: HOOK — one shocking fact or question that makes the viewer stop scrolling
  Scene 2: SETUP — what is the phenomenon? describe the real-world context
  Middle scenes: BUILD — explain the physics step by step, each scene advances the story
  Second-to-last scene: CLIMAX — the key insight, the moment everything clicks
  Last scene: CONCLUSION — a satisfying one-sentence payoff that answers the hook
- The LAST sentence must directly answer or resolve the FIRST sentence (hook → resolution)
- Every scene narration_segment must feel like a continuation — no abrupt topic jumps
- Each sentence must describe exactly what the viewer is WATCHING in that scene
- Conversational but precise — explain jargon the moment you use it
- NEVER use vague phrases like "incredible", "amazing", "mind-blowing" — use specific physics facts
- NEVER end mid-sentence or mid-thought — the story must feel complete and satisfying
VISUAL PROMPT RULES (CRITICAL — read every rule):
Each scene needs ONE visual_prompt. These go directly into Seedance, an AI video generator.
Seedance produces best results with EXPLICIT 3D CGI ANIMATION STYLE descriptions.
The viewer watches the visual while listening to the narration_segment — they MUST match.

MANDATORY FORMAT for every visual_prompt:
"3D CGI educational physics animation, [SPECIFIC PHYSICS OBJECT/APPARATUS with exact physical description — size, material, colour, texture], [SPECIFIC PHYSICS ACTION happening with precise detail], [camera movement that follows the action], [lighting — ray-traced/studio/ambient], Blender/Pixar render quality, photorealistic materials, 9:16 vertical, deep black background, cinematic depth of field, no text, no people, no UI"

RULE 1 — DESCRIBE THE REAL 3D OBJECT, NOT THE CONCEPT:
- Describe the actual physical apparatus as a 3D rendered object
- Oil drop experiment → "two horizontal polished brass electrodes 5cm apart, a tiny transparent oil droplet 0.002mm diameter floating between them"
- Double slit → "a thin aluminium barrier with two parallel slits 0.05mm wide and 0.25mm apart"
- Electric field → "glowing amber field lines curving from positive red plate to negative blue plate"
- NOT: "electric force" or "charge packet" — describe what it LOOKS LIKE as a 3D object

RULE 2 — PHYSICS ACTION MUST BE VISIBLE AND SPECIFIC:
- The action must be something a 3D render can show
- "droplet slowly drifts upward at 0.05mm/s as voltage increases"
- "red laser beam refracts at 32 degrees entering glass block surface"
- "two wave crests collide forming a peak twice the amplitude"
- "electron beam curves into a circular arc inside magnetic field coil"

RULE 3 — CAMERA FOLLOWS THE PHYSICS:
- "camera slow-zooms into the droplet from 10cm away to 1mm macro distance"
- "camera orbits 180 degrees around the apparatus at eye level"
- "camera tracks the wave packet moving left to right at 1/4 speed"
- "top-down camera slowly rotates revealing the interference pattern"

RULE 4 — LIGHTING AND RENDER QUALITY:
- Always specify: "ray-traced lighting, photorealistic materials"
- Use: "studio three-point lighting with blue-tinted key light"
- Or: "single dramatic spotlight from above casting sharp shadows"
- Metals should look like metal: "brushed aluminium", "polished brass", "chrome"
- Glass/liquids: "caustic light refraction through glass", "subsurface scattering on droplet"

RULE 5 — COLOUR PALETTE (apply to every prompt):
- Background: deep matte black
- Positive charge / electric field: warm amber / orange glow
- Negative charge / electron: cool cyan / electric blue
- Magnetic field: purple / violet
- Neutral objects: brushed metal silver, matte grey
- Energy/photons: bright white → yellow gradient trail

RULE 6 — MANDATORY CAMERA MOVEMENT IN EVERY CLIP:
Every 5-second clip MUST have camera motion — a static shot is forbidden.
The camera must start at one position and end at a clearly different position.
Required: specify START position and END position of the camera.
Examples of good camera moves for 5 seconds:
- "camera starts 10cm from apparatus and slow-zooms to 2cm macro close-up over 5 seconds"
- "camera begins at front angle and orbits 60 degrees to side profile over 5 seconds"
- "camera starts overhead and tilts down 45 degrees tracking the falling object"
- "camera tracks horizontally left-to-right following the moving particle"
- "camera begins wide showing full apparatus then slowly pushes in toward the key action"
The camera move must follow and REVEAL the physics action — not just drift randomly.

RULE 7 — VISUAL CONTINUITY ACROSS ALL CLIPS:
All clips must feel like consecutive shots in ONE continuous scene — not random separate videos.
Rules for continuity:
- Same apparatus appears in every clip — same brass plates, same chamber, same objects
- Each clip zooms IN progressively: Clip 1 = wide establishing shot, middle clips = medium close-up of action, final clip = extreme close-up of result
- Lighting stays consistent across all clips — same colour temperature, same shadow direction
- If an object moves in Clip N, Clip N+1 shows it after it has moved (cause → effect sequence)
- Colour palette stays identical — same amber for positive, same cyan for negative across all clips
When writing the scenes array, mentally check: if you played all clips back-to-back, does it tell a single visual story of the physics experiment from start to finish?

EXPLICITLY FORBIDDEN:
- "glowing particle trails" (too generic — describe the specific glow)
- "scientific documentary grade" (conflicts with CGI style)
- "physics simulation" (vague — say "3D CGI educational animation")
- People, hands, faces, text overlays, equations on screen
- Generic phrases: "high contrast", "dark laboratory", "cinematic" alone without specifics

HIGH QUALITY EXAMPLES (Seedance responds well to these):

Example 1 — Millikan Oil Drop:
"3D CGI educational physics animation, two horizontal polished brass electrodes 5cm apart inside a glass-walled vacuum chamber, a single transparent oil droplet 0.002mm diameter with faint blue surface charge glow floating motionless between the plates, warm amber voltage glow on upper plate, cool cyan glow on lower plate, camera slow-orbits 90 degrees around the droplet at eye level, ray-traced lighting with soft studio fill, photorealistic glass and metal materials, Blender render quality, 9:16 vertical, deep black background, cinematic depth of field, no text, no people"

Example 2 — Double Slit Interference:
"3D CGI educational physics animation, thin brushed aluminium barrier with two parallel vertical slits 0.05mm wide, coherent 650nm red laser beam hitting the barrier and diffracting through both slits simultaneously, two expanding semicircular wave fronts emerging from slits and interfering — bright red constructive bands and dark destructive bands spreading onto a white screen 1m behind, camera starts behind the laser and slow-zooms through the slits toward the screen, ray-traced volumetric light scattering, photorealistic metal and glass, Blender/Pixar quality, 9:16 vertical, deep black background, no text, no people"

Example 3 — Magnetic Force on Current:
"3D CGI educational physics animation, straight copper wire suspended horizontally between two ceramic magnets (north pole red, south pole blue) with visible purple magnetic field lines arcing between poles, electric current flowing through wire causes wire to deflect upward 15 degrees, slow-motion deflection captured in extreme close-up, camera tracks the wire deflection from 2cm distance, dramatic single overhead spotlight with sharp shadows, photorealistic copper metal material with warm electrical glow, Blender render quality, 9:16 vertical, deep black background, no text, no people"

Example 4 — Wave Interference:
"3D CGI educational physics animation, two identical point sources on a dark water surface generating circular ripples with 2cm wavelength, ripples from both sources overlapping — constructive interference creating peaks twice normal height shown in bright white, destructive interference creating flat nodes shown in dark grey, viewed from directly overhead, camera slowly zooms out from one source to reveal full interference pattern, caustic light simulation through ripple surfaces, photorealistic water material with subsurface scattering, Blender render quality, 9:16 vertical, deep black background, no text, no people"

NARRATION-VISUAL SYNC (strict):
- Each narration_segment = the EXACT words spoken while that scene plays
- Sum of all narration_segments must equal the full narration word-for-word
- The visual_prompt must show ONLY what the narration_segment describes
- If narration says "now we see X" — the visual shows X, not something related to X

' +
'══════════════════════════════════════════
Return ONLY a raw JSON object (no markdown, no extra text):

{
  "narration": "<full narration, ~' + word_target + ' words>",
  "scenes": [
    {
      "scene_number": 1,
      "start_sec": 0,
      "end_sec": 5,
      "narration_segment": "<exact words spoken during this scene>",
      "visual_prompt": "3D CGI educational physics animation, <specific 3D object/apparatus with exact physical description>, <specific physics action with precise detail>, <camera movement following the action>, ray-traced lighting, photorealistic materials, Blender/Pixar render quality, 9:16 vertical, deep black background, cinematic depth of field, no text, no people"
    }
    ... (repeat for all ' + num_clips + ' scenes)
  ],
  "title": "<YouTube title, under 60 chars, curiosity-driven, names the specific physics phenomenon>",
  "hashtags": "#physics #science #shorts #learnphysics"
}';

return [{ json: { ...data, gemini_script_request: {
  contents: [{ parts: [{ text: prompt }] }],
  generationConfig: {
    maxOutputTokens: 8192,
    temperature: 0.75,
    responseMimeType: 'application/json',
    thinkingConfig: { thinkingBudget: 0 }
  }
}}}];

const data = $input.first().json;
return [{ json: {
  job_id: data.job_id,
  topic: data.topic,
  title: data.title,
  hashtags: data.hashtags,
  narration: data.narration,
  clip_prompts: data.clip_prompts,
  audio_path: data.audio_path,
  subs_path: data.subs_path,
  audio_duration: data.audio_duration,
  subtitle_chunks: data.subtitle_chunks,
  word_timings: data.word_timings,
  sheet_row: data.sheet_row,
  start_time: data.start_time
} }];

parameters.jsonBody: ={{ JSON.stringify({ contents: [{ parts: [{ text: 'You are a physics expert. Research this topic for a physics YouTube video: ' + $json.topic + '. Answer: 1) What does this phenomenon LOOK LIKE visually? (colors, shapes, movement, scale, glow, sparks, waves etc) 2) What is the key experiment setup? (equipment, lab setup, what is visible) 3) What are the 3 most visually striking moments? 4) What measurable quantities are visible? (wavelengths, voltages, angles, speeds) 5) Best camera angle to show this? Be specific, scientific, visual. 200-300 words, plain text.' }] }], generationConfig: { maxOutputTokens: 1024, temperature: 0.3 } }) }}
